1	You are challenging a decision proposal. Do not edit files.
2	Your objective is not to approve the proposal or help it reach approval.
3	Attempt to falsify the proposal, your own strongest objection, and the strongest viable alternative.
4	Treat proposer and reviewer as equally fallible. Do not preserve an objection merely for consistency.
5	Every factual or invariant finding must identify evidence, its causal boundary, and what would falsify it.
6	A preference is not a finding. A tradeoff must cite an explicit decision criterion.
7	Recommend retaining the original proposal when objections do not survive evidence.
8	Recommend abandonment when revision would only preserve sunk cost.
9	Return only the requested structured challenge output. Never return LGTM.
10	
11	## Decision Needed
12	Decide whether Pi Suite should remove the local container preflight from the
13	release path and make GitHub Actions the sole validation and promotion control
14	plane.
15	
16	## Observed Facts
17	At dossier creation time:
18	
19	- `.github/workflows/release.yml` runs on pushes to `main` and pull requests.
20	- The candidate job installs dependencies, validates the release source, builds
21	  or restores candidates, verifies candidates, and uploads an artifact.
22	- macOS and Windows jobs witness the uploaded candidate.
23	- The publish job runs only after candidate and platform witnesses, prepares a
24	  release record, may push a release commit and tags to `main`, persists the
25	  archive, and publishes it.
26	- The workflow does not have a job that runs the root `pnpm verify` command.
27	- The workflow does not run `pnpm verify:consumers`.
28	- The local `release:preflight` currently supplies broader checks before
29	  `push:release` changes the remote release path.
30	- Candidate artifacts currently have a finite retention period.
31	
32	These observations must be refreshed against the exact implementation
33	candidate before a final gate.
34	
35	## Decision Criteria
36	1. An unverified SHA cannot reach `main` through a supported path.
37	2. An unwitnessed or rebuilt archive cannot be published.
38	3. All checks currently supplied by local preflight remain represented in CI,
39	   including `pnpm verify`, candidate verification, consumer verification,
40	   Chrome candidate/smoke coverage, and platform witnesses.
41	4. Promotion is fail closed under concurrent submissions or movement of
42	   `main`.
43	5. Candidate code cannot gain promotion credentials merely by running in the
44	   validation workflow.
45	6. Local blocking time is reduced to source submission time.
46	7. Release-intent-to-publication latency is minimized without weakening source
47	   or archive identity.
48	8. The workflow remains diagnosable and reproducible when GitHub-hosted CI
49	   fails.
50	9. One release source SHA still maps to one explicit public-package release set
51	   and one exact archive per selected package.
52	
53	## Competing Alternatives
54	### A. Retain mandatory local container preflight
55	
56	Continue validating locally before pushing `main`, then repeat platform and
57	publication work in Actions.
58	
59	- Strength: catches failures before any remote mutation and provides a local
60	  Linux reproduction environment.
61	- Weakness: duplicates CI work, blocks the operator, depends on local container
62	  runtime behavior, and still does not make the remote control plane complete
63	  by construction.
64	
65	### B. Remove local preflight but keep push-to-main release
66	
67	Add missing checks to the existing workflow while allowing the source SHA to
68	enter `main` before they complete.
69	
70	- Strength: smallest workflow edit.
71	- Weakness: creates a period where `main` contains an unverified release source;
72	  rollback or follow-up repair becomes part of the safety model.
73	
74	### C. Validate candidate ref, then promote exact SHA
75	
76	Run the complete witness DAG on a candidate ref and fast-forward `main` only
77	after success.
78	
79	- Strength: closes the unverified-main window and removes local heavy gates.
80	- Weakness: requires branch protection, serialized promotion, durable artifact
81	  identity, and a privilege boundary between validation and promotion.
82	
83	### D. Continuously witness every eligible candidate SHA
84	
85	Use C, but start the complete release witness on each eligible final candidate
86	push so release intent only promotes and publishes an existing witness.
87	
88	- Strength: minimizes release-intent latency.
89	- Weakness: consumes more Actions capacity, can build artifacts never released,
90	  and requires an explicit policy for witness expiry and stale candidates.
91	
92	### E. Dedicated self-hosted Actions runners
93	
94	Use C or D with persistent or ephemeral self-hosted runners.
95	
96	- Strength: may reduce queue, checkout, and install latency.
97	- Weakness: adds runner isolation, credential, cleanup, availability, and
98	  maintenance responsibilities. Persistent runners can leak state between
99	  candidate executions.
100	
101	## Proposed Change
102	The proposed candidate is:
103	
104	1. A committed source SHA is submitted to a candidate ref without changing
105	   `main`.
106	2. GitHub Actions validates that exact SHA, builds one candidate archive, and
107	   records the archive digest.
108	3. Quality, consumer, Chrome, macOS, and Windows witnesses validate the source
109	   SHA or that exact archive as appropriate.
110	4. A separate privileged promoter verifies the completed witness set without
111	   executing candidate code, then fast-forwards `main` to the exact source SHA.
112	5. Publication consumes the witnessed archive without rebuilding or repacking.
113	6. Local release submission performs no dependency install, build, test, or
114	   container execution. It may reject an uncommitted or non-ancestral source
115	   because those are source-identity protocol errors, not validation gates.
116	7. The existing container preflight remains only as an optional Linux
117	   reproduction command.
118	
119	An optimized form continuously creates a release witness for every eligible
120	final candidate SHA. A later release request reuses that exact witness and
121	archive rather than starting validation at release time.
122	
123	## Stable axis, change axis, and invariant
124	
125	- Stable axis: committed source SHA, selected public release set, frozen
126	  lockfile, candidate archive bytes, supported platforms, npm Trusted
127	  Publishing, and public-registry integrity acceptance.
128	- Change axis: where validation executes, when validation begins, the CI job
129	  DAG, and how a witnessed SHA is promoted to `main`.
130	- Invariant: only an exact source SHA with a complete witness set may enter
131	  `main`, and npm may receive only the exact archive bytes bound to that
132	  witness. No validation result may be reused across a different SHA or
133	  archive digest.
134	
135	## Known Weaknesses
136	1. GitHub Actions availability and queue latency become release-path
137	   dependencies.
138	2. A privileged `workflow_run`-style promoter is dangerous if it checks out or
139	   executes candidate-controlled code, trusts artifact names without digest
140	   binding, or accepts a result from the wrong workflow or repository context.
141	3. Fast-forward promotion is insufficient if another supported path can push
142	   directly to `main` or create release tags.
143	4. Finite artifact retention conflicts with indefinite reuse of a previously
144	   witnessed release candidate.
145	5. A release-record commit created after validation has a different SHA from
146	   the witnessed source. The design must distinguish source SHA, release-record
147	   SHA, tag target, and published archive identity without claiming they are one
148	   value.
149	6. Cancellation and concurrency rules can cancel a valid candidate, promote a
150	   stale candidate, or interleave irreversible publication unless promotion and
151	   publication have explicit serialization semantics.
152	7. Aggressive affected-package selection can omit a required witness when root
153	   tooling, shared contracts, lockfile state, generated assets, or transitive
154	   consumers change.
155	8. Splitting checks into too many jobs may increase wall time because each job
156	   repeats queueing, checkout, setup, and installation.
157	9. Consumer and browser smoke tests may depend on services or hardware not
158	   faithfully represented on all GitHub-hosted runners.
159	10. Reusing caches must not turn mutable build output into evidence or weaken
160	    frozen-lockfile and archive-integrity verification.
161	
162	## Required evidence before retention
163	
164	1. A command-to-job coverage map proving every mandatory local preflight check
165	   has an authoritative CI owner or is explicitly removed with justification.
166	2. A source/archive identity model covering source SHA, release-record commit,
167	   tag, candidate manifest, artifact digest, npm integrity, and registry
168	   acceptance.
169	3. Repository rules or equivalent enforcement showing that supported writes to
170	   `main` and release tags pass through the promoter.
171	4. A threat analysis for unprivileged validation versus privileged promotion,
172	   including artifact retrieval and metadata validation.
173	5. A concurrency model for multiple candidate SHAs, retries, cancellation,
174	   partial publish, and movement of `main`.
175	6. A retention model defining when a witness expires and whether the archive is
176	   persisted to a durable immutable location before promotion/publication.
177	7. Measured Actions step timings showing whether job sharding, combining, or
178	   runner changes reduce the actual critical path.
179	8. Tests that attempt direct-main release, digest substitution, wrong-run
180	   artifact reuse, stale promotion, rebuild-before-publish, and missing witness
181	   promotion, and observe fail-closed behavior.
182	
183	## Abandon Conditions
184	Abandon the CI-only proposal if any of these remain true:
185	
186	1. The repository cannot prevent supported direct writes to `main` or release
187	   tags that bypass the witness gate.
188	2. The promoter must execute candidate-controlled code while holding write or
189	   publication authority.
190	3. The publication path cannot consume the exact archive witnessed by all
191	   required jobs.
192	4. Required release checks cannot run reliably in GitHub Actions and no
193	   equivalent remote witness is available.
194	5. The organization requires release capability during GitHub Actions outages
195	   and accepts neither delayed release nor a separately audited emergency path.
196	
197	## Evidence That Would Change Our Mind
198	- If measured Actions queue plus execution latency is consistently worse than
199	  the local preflight and release urgency requires synchronous completion,
200	  retain remote gating but reconsider continuous witnesses or runner capacity.
201	- If branch rules and promoter credentials cannot enforce exclusive promotion,
202	  retain mandatory local checks only as mitigation while redesigning the remote
203	  ownership boundary; do not claim the class is closed.
204	- If continuous witnesses consume disproportionate capacity relative to actual
205	  releases, choose on-demand candidate-ref validation instead of D.
206	- If a dedicated ephemeral runner materially shortens the measured critical
207	  path without introducing persistent trust state, reconsider E.
208	- If release-record mutation cannot be separated from the witnessed source and
209	  archive identities, revise the release-record model before removing the local
210	  gate.
211	
212	## Assumptions
213	1. GitHub Actions remains the authorized remote execution environment for this
214	   repository and npm Trusted Publishing remains available there.
215	2. Delaying a release during a GitHub Actions outage is acceptable unless a
216	   separately audited emergency policy is introduced.
217	3. Repository rules or an equivalent GitHub enforcement mechanism can make the
218	   promoter the only supported writer to `main` and release tags.
219	4. The release candidate can be persisted and addressed by source SHA plus
220	   cryptographic digest.
221	
222	## Non-Goals
223	1. Making untrusted third-party code safe on a persistent self-hosted runner.
224	2. Supporting an undocumented manual publication bypass.
225	3. Reusing mutable build directories, `node_modules`, or prior test results as
226	   release evidence.
227	4. Guaranteeing releases during a GitHub-wide control-plane outage.
228	
229	## Review Questions
230	1. Does candidate-ref validation plus privileged promotion actually close the
231	   unverified-main failure class under the current GitHub permission model?
232	2. Can release-record creation preserve the distinction between witnessed
233	   source identity and the later release metadata commit?
234	3. Is continuous witnessing justified, or does on-demand candidate validation
235	   dominate after Actions cost and candidate frequency are measured?
236	4. Which checks are safe to parallelize without introducing a second build or
237	   a second source of candidate truth?
238	5. What is the strongest viable alternative if exclusive promotion cannot be
239	   enforced?
240	
241	## Initial Recommendation
242	
243	Prefer C as the safety architecture. Add D only for branches or explicit
244	candidate refs likely to be released. Do not make E part of the release
245	contract; use hosted runners first and introduce ephemeral self-hosted capacity
246	only after measurements identify queue or runner speed as the dominant cost.

## Decision Needed
Decide whether Pi Suite should remove the local container preflight from the
release path and make GitHub Actions the sole validation and promotion control
plane.

## Observed Facts
At dossier creation time:

- `.github/workflows/release.yml` runs on pushes to `main` and pull requests.
- The candidate job installs dependencies, validates the release source, builds
  or restores candidates, verifies candidates, and uploads an artifact.
- macOS and Windows jobs witness the uploaded candidate.
- The publish job runs only after candidate and platform witnesses, prepares a
  release record, may push a release commit and tags to `main`, persists the
  archive, and publishes it.
- The workflow does not have a job that runs the root `pnpm verify` command.
- The workflow does not run `pnpm verify:consumers`.
- The local `release:preflight` currently supplies broader checks before
  `push:release` changes the remote release path.
- Candidate artifacts currently have a finite retention period.

These observations must be refreshed against the exact implementation
candidate before a final gate.

## Decision Criteria
1. An unverified SHA cannot reach `main` through a supported path.
2. An unwitnessed or rebuilt archive cannot be published.
3. All checks currently supplied by local preflight remain represented in CI,
   including `pnpm verify`, candidate verification, consumer verification,
   Chrome candidate/smoke coverage, and platform witnesses.
4. Promotion is fail closed under concurrent submissions or movement of
   `main`.
5. Candidate code cannot gain promotion credentials merely by running in the
   validation workflow.
6. Local blocking time is reduced to source submission time.
7. Release-intent-to-publication latency is minimized without weakening source
   or archive identity.
8. The workflow remains diagnosable and reproducible when GitHub-hosted CI
   fails.
9. One release source SHA still maps to one explicit public-package release set
   and one exact archive per selected package.

## Competing Alternatives
### A. Retain mandatory local container preflight

Continue validating locally before pushing `main`, then repeat platform and
publication work in Actions.

- Strength: catches failures before any remote mutation and provides a local
  Linux reproduction environment.
- Weakness: duplicates CI work, blocks the operator, depends on local container
  runtime behavior, and still does not make the remote control plane complete
  by construction.

### B. Remove local preflight but keep push-to-main release

Add missing checks to the existing workflow while allowing the source SHA to
enter `main` before they complete.

- Strength: smallest workflow edit.
- Weakness: creates a period where `main` contains an unverified release source;
  rollback or follow-up repair becomes part of the safety model.

### C. Validate candidate ref, then promote exact SHA

Run the complete witness DAG on a candidate ref and fast-forward `main` only
after success.

- Strength: closes the unverified-main window and removes local heavy gates.
- Weakness: requires branch protection, serialized promotion, durable artifact
  identity, and a privilege boundary between validation and promotion.

### D. Continuously witness every eligible candidate SHA

Use C, but start the complete release witness on each eligible final candidate
push so release intent only promotes and publishes an existing witness.

- Strength: minimizes release-intent latency.
- Weakness: consumes more Actions capacity, can build artifacts never released,
  and requires an explicit policy for witness expiry and stale candidates.

### E. Dedicated self-hosted Actions runners

Use C or D with persistent or ephemeral self-hosted runners.

- Strength: may reduce queue, checkout, and install latency.
- Weakness: adds runner isolation, credential, cleanup, availability, and
  maintenance responsibilities. Persistent runners can leak state between
  candidate executions.

## Proposed Change
The proposed candidate is:

1. A committed source SHA is submitted to a candidate ref without changing
   `main`.
2. GitHub Actions validates that exact SHA, builds one candidate archive, and
   records the archive digest.
3. Quality, consumer, Chrome, macOS, and Windows witnesses validate the source
   SHA or that exact archive as appropriate.
4. A separate privileged promoter verifies the completed witness set without
   executing candidate code, then fast-forwards `main` to the exact source SHA.
5. Publication consumes the witnessed archive without rebuilding or repacking.
6. Local release submission performs no dependency install, build, test, or
   container execution. It may reject an uncommitted or non-ancestral source
   because those are source-identity protocol errors, not validation gates.
7. The existing container preflight remains only as an optional Linux
   reproduction command.

An optimized form continuously creates a release witness for every eligible
final candidate SHA. A later release request reuses that exact witness and
archive rather than starting validation at release time.

## Stable axis, change axis, and invariant

- Stable axis: committed source SHA, selected public release set, frozen
  lockfile, candidate archive bytes, supported platforms, npm Trusted
  Publishing, and public-registry integrity acceptance.
- Change axis: where validation executes, when validation begins, the CI job
  DAG, and how a witnessed SHA is promoted to `main`.
- Invariant: only an exact source SHA with a complete witness set may enter
  `main`, and npm may receive only the exact archive bytes bound to that
  witness. No validation result may be reused across a different SHA or
  archive digest.

## Known Weaknesses
1. GitHub Actions availability and queue latency become release-path
   dependencies.
2. A privileged `workflow_run`-style promoter is dangerous if it checks out or
   executes candidate-controlled code, trusts artifact names without digest
   binding, or accepts a result from the wrong workflow or repository context.
3. Fast-forward promotion is insufficient if another supported path can push
   directly to `main` or create release tags.
4. Finite artifact retention conflicts with indefinite reuse of a previously
   witnessed release candidate.
5. A release-record commit created after validation has a different SHA from
   the witnessed source. The design must distinguish source SHA, release-record
   SHA, tag target, and published archive identity without claiming they are one
   value.
6. Cancellation and concurrency rules can cancel a valid candidate, promote a
   stale candidate, or interleave irreversible publication unless promotion and
   publication have explicit serialization semantics.
7. Aggressive affected-package selection can omit a required witness when root
   tooling, shared contracts, lockfile state, generated assets, or transitive
   consumers change.
8. Splitting checks into too many jobs may increase wall time because each job
   repeats queueing, checkout, setup, and installation.
9. Consumer and browser smoke tests may depend on services or hardware not
   faithfully represented on all GitHub-hosted runners.
10. Reusing caches must not turn mutable build output into evidence or weaken
    frozen-lockfile and archive-integrity verification.

## Required evidence before retention

1. A command-to-job coverage map proving every mandatory local preflight check
   has an authoritative CI owner or is explicitly removed with justification.
2. A source/archive identity model covering source SHA, release-record commit,
   tag, candidate manifest, artifact digest, npm integrity, and registry
   acceptance.
3. Repository rules or equivalent enforcement showing that supported writes to
   `main` and release tags pass through the promoter.
4. A threat analysis for unprivileged validation versus privileged promotion,
   including artifact retrieval and metadata validation.
5. A concurrency model for multiple candidate SHAs, retries, cancellation,
   partial publish, and movement of `main`.
6. A retention model defining when a witness expires and whether the archive is
   persisted to a durable immutable location before promotion/publication.
7. Measured Actions step timings showing whether job sharding, combining, or
   runner changes reduce the actual critical path.
8. Tests that attempt direct-main release, digest substitution, wrong-run
   artifact reuse, stale promotion, rebuild-before-publish, and missing witness
   promotion, and observe fail-closed behavior.

## Abandon Conditions
Abandon the CI-only proposal if any of these remain true:

1. The repository cannot prevent supported direct writes to `main` or release
   tags that bypass the witness gate.
2. The promoter must execute candidate-controlled code while holding write or
   publication authority.
3. The publication path cannot consume the exact archive witnessed by all
   required jobs.
4. Required release checks cannot run reliably in GitHub Actions and no
   equivalent remote witness is available.
5. The organization requires release capability during GitHub Actions outages
   and accepts neither delayed release nor a separately audited emergency path.

## Evidence That Would Change Our Mind
- If measured Actions queue plus execution latency is consistently worse than
  the local preflight and release urgency requires synchronous completion,
  retain remote gating but reconsider continuous witnesses or runner capacity.
- If branch rules and promoter credentials cannot enforce exclusive promotion,
  retain mandatory local checks only as mitigation while redesigning the remote
  ownership boundary; do not claim the class is closed.
- If continuous witnesses consume disproportionate capacity relative to actual
  releases, choose on-demand candidate-ref validation instead of D.
- If a dedicated ephemeral runner materially shortens the measured critical
  path without introducing persistent trust state, reconsider E.
- If release-record mutation cannot be separated from the witnessed source and
  archive identities, revise the release-record model before removing the local
  gate.

## Assumptions
1. GitHub Actions remains the authorized remote execution environment for this
   repository and npm Trusted Publishing remains available there.
2. Delaying a release during a GitHub Actions outage is acceptable unless a
   separately audited emergency policy is introduced.
3. Repository rules or an equivalent GitHub enforcement mechanism can make the
   promoter the only supported writer to `main` and release tags.
4. The release candidate can be persisted and addressed by source SHA plus
   cryptographic digest.

## Non-Goals
1. Making untrusted third-party code safe on a persistent self-hosted runner.
2. Supporting an undocumented manual publication bypass.
3. Reusing mutable build directories, `node_modules`, or prior test results as
   release evidence.
4. Guaranteeing releases during a GitHub-wide control-plane outage.

## Review Questions
1. Does candidate-ref validation plus privileged promotion actually close the
   unverified-main failure class under the current GitHub permission model?
2. Can release-record creation preserve the distinction between witnessed
   source identity and the later release metadata commit?
3. Is continuous witnessing justified, or does on-demand candidate validation
   dominate after Actions cost and candidate frequency are measured?
4. Which checks are safe to parallelize without introducing a second build or
   a second source of candidate truth?
5. What is the strongest viable alternative if exclusive promotion cannot be
   enforced?

## Initial Recommendation

Prefer C as the safety architecture. Add D only for branches or explicit
candidate refs likely to be released. Do not make E part of the release
contract; use hosted runners first and introduce ephemeral self-hosted capacity
only after measurements identify queue or runner speed as the dominant cost.

1	name: Release Suite
2	
3	on:
4	  push:
5	    branches: [main]
6	  pull_request:
7	
8	concurrency:
9	  group: ${{ github.event_name == 'push' && 'pi-suite-release-main' || format('pi-suite-release-pr-{0}', github.event.pull_request.number) }}
10	  queue: max
11	  cancel-in-progress: false
12	
13	jobs:
14	  classify:
15	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
16	    outputs:
17	      release_commit: ${{ steps.commit.outputs.release_commit }}
18	      source_release: ${{ steps.commit.outputs.source_release }}
19	    steps:
20	      - uses: actions/checkout@v5
21	        with:
22	          ref: ${{ github.sha }}
23	          fetch-depth: 2
24	      - id: commit
25	        run: node tooling/release/classify.mjs "${{ github.sha }}"
26	
27	  candidate:
28	    needs: classify
29	    if: needs.classify.outputs.release_commit == 'false' && needs.classify.outputs.source_release == 'true'
30	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
31	    timeout-minutes: 45
32	    permissions:
33	      contents: read
34	      actions: read
35	    outputs:
36	      mode: ${{ steps.source.outputs.mode }}
37	      release_tag: ${{ steps.source.outputs.release_tag }}
38	      artifact: ${{ steps.artifact.outputs.name }}
39	    steps:
40	      - uses: actions/checkout@v5
41	        with:
42	          ref: ${{ github.sha }}
43	          fetch-depth: 0
44	      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v5
45	      - uses: actions/setup-node@v5
46	        with:
47	          node-version: 24.11.0
48	          cache: pnpm
49	      - run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
50	      - id: source
51	        env:
52	          PI_SUITE_RELEASE_PREVIEW: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' && '1' || '0' }}
53	        run: node tooling/release/candidate-pipeline.mjs verify-release-source "${{ github.sha }}"
54	      - name: Materialize the one candidate owned by this source
55	        shell: bash
56	        env:
57	          GH_TOKEN: ${{ github.token }}
58	        run: |
59	          restore_prior_attempt() {
60	            rm -rf release/restore release/assets
61	            mkdir -p release/restore release/assets
62	            gh run download "$GITHUB_RUN_ID" --name "$prior_artifact" --dir release/restore
63	            stored="$(find release/restore -type f -name "$asset" -print -quit)"
64	            test -n "$stored"
65	            cp "$stored" "release/assets/$asset"
66	            node tooling/release/candidate-store.mjs restore "${{ github.sha }}" "release/assets/$asset"
67	          }
68	
69	          if [ "${{ github.event_name }}" = "pull_request" ]; then
70	            node tooling/release/candidate-pipeline.mjs build
71	          else
72	            asset="suite-candidates-${{ github.sha }}.tgz"
73	            prior_artifact="$(
74	              gh api "repos/$GITHUB_REPOSITORY/actions/runs/$GITHUB_RUN_ID/artifacts?per_page=100" \
75	                --jq '.artifacts | map(select(.expired == false and (.name | startswith("suite-candidates-'"$GITHUB_RUN_ID"'-")))) | sort_by(.id) | last | .name // ""'
76	            )"
77	            if [ "${{ steps.source.outputs.mode }}" = "existing" ]; then
78	              rm -rf release/restore release/assets
79	              mkdir -p release/restore release/assets
80	              if gh release download "${{ steps.source.outputs.release_tag }}" \
81	                --pattern "$asset" \
82	                --dir release/restore; then
83	                stored="release/restore/$asset"
84	                cp "$stored" "release/assets/$asset"
85	                node tooling/release/candidate-store.mjs restore "${{ github.sha }}" "release/assets/$asset"
86	              elif [ -n "$prior_artifact" ]; then
87	                restore_prior_attempt
88	              else
89	                echo "The existing release has no durable or prior-attempt candidate." >&2
90	                exit 1
91	              fi
92	            elif [ -n "$prior_artifact" ]; then
93	              restore_prior_attempt
94	            else
95	              node tooling/release/candidate-pipeline.mjs build "${{ github.sha }}"
96	              node tooling/release/candidate-store.mjs bundle "${{ github.sha }}"
97	            fi
98	          fi
99	      - run: node tooling/release/candidate-pipeline.mjs verify-candidate
100	      - id: artifact
101	        shell: bash
102	        run: echo "name=suite-candidates-${GITHUB_RUN_ID}-${GITHUB_RUN_ATTEMPT}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
103	      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v6
104	        with:
105	          name: ${{ steps.artifact.outputs.name }}
106	          path: |
107	            release/candidate.json
108	            release/candidates/*.tgz
109	            release/assets/suite-candidates-${{ github.sha }}.tgz
110	          if-no-files-found: error
111	          retention-days: 14
112

name: Release Suite

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:

concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.event_name == 'push' && 'pi-suite-release-main' || format('pi-suite-release-pr-{0}', github.event.pull_request.number) }}
  queue: max
  cancel-in-progress: false

jobs:
  classify:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      release_commit: ${{ steps.commit.outputs.release_commit }}
      source_release: ${{ steps.commit.outputs.source_release }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v5
        with:
          ref: ${{ github.sha }}
          fetch-depth: 2
      - id: commit
        run: node tooling/release/classify.mjs "${{ github.sha }}"

  candidate:
    needs: classify
    if: needs.classify.outputs.release_commit == 'false' && needs.classify.outputs.source_release == 'true'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 45
    permissions:
      contents: read
      actions: read
    outputs:
      mode: ${{ steps.source.outputs.mode }}
      release_tag: ${{ steps.source.outputs.release_tag }}
      artifact: ${{ steps.artifact.outputs.name }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v5
        with:
          ref: ${{ github.sha }}
          fetch-depth: 0
      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v5
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v5
        with:
          node-version: 24.11.0
          cache: pnpm
      - run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
      - id: source
        env:
          PI_SUITE_RELEASE_PREVIEW: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' && '1' || '0' }}
        run: node tooling/release/candidate-pipeline.mjs verify-release-source "${{ github.sha }}"
      - name: Materialize the one candidate owned by this source
        shell: bash
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ github.token }}
        run: |
          restore_prior_attempt() {
            rm -rf release/restore release/assets
            mkdir -p release/restore release/assets
            gh run download "$GITHUB_RUN_ID" --name "$prior_artifact" --dir release/restore
            stored="$(find release/restore -type f -name "$asset" -print -quit)"
            test -n "$stored"
            cp "$stored" "release/assets/$asset"
            node tooling/release/candidate-store.mjs restore "${{ github.sha }}" "release/assets/$asset"
          }

          if [ "${{ github.event_name }}" = "pull_request" ]; then
            node tooling/release/candidate-pipeline.mjs build
          else
            asset="suite-candidates-${{ github.sha }}.tgz"
            prior_artifact="$(
              gh api "repos/$GITHUB_REPOSITORY/actions/runs/$GITHUB_RUN_ID/artifacts?per_page=100" \
                --jq '.artifacts | map(select(.expired == false and (.name | startswith("suite-candidates-'"$GITHUB_RUN_ID"'-")))) | sort_by(.id) | last | .name // ""'
            )"
            if [ "${{ steps.source.outputs.mode }}" = "existing" ]; then
              rm -rf release/restore release/assets
              mkdir -p release/restore release/assets
              if gh release download "${{ steps.source.outputs.release_tag }}" \
                --pattern "$asset" \
                --dir release/restore; then
                stored="release/restore/$asset"
                cp "$stored" "release/assets/$asset"
                node tooling/release/candidate-store.mjs restore "${{ github.sha }}" "release/assets/$asset"
              elif [ -n "$prior_artifact" ]; then
                restore_prior_attempt
              else
                echo "The existing release has no durable or prior-attempt candidate." >&2
                exit 1
              fi
            elif [ -n "$prior_artifact" ]; then
              restore_prior_attempt
            else
              node tooling/release/candidate-pipeline.mjs build "${{ github.sha }}"
              node tooling/release/candidate-store.mjs bundle "${{ github.sha }}"
            fi
          fi
      - run: node tooling/release/candidate-pipeline.mjs verify-candidate
      - id: artifact
        shell: bash
        run: echo "name=suite-candidates-${GITHUB_RUN_ID}-${GITHUB_RUN_ATTEMPT}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v6
        with:
          name: ${{ steps.artifact.outputs.name }}
          path: |
            release/candidate.json
            release/candidates/*.tgz
            release/assets/suite-candidates-${{ github.sha }}.tgz
          if-no-files-found: error
          retention-days: 14

  platform-witness:
    needs: candidate
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        os: [macos-14, windows-2022]
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    timeout-minutes: 30
    permissions:
      contents: read
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v5
        with:
          ref: ${{ github.sha }}
      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v5
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v5
        with:
          node-version: 24.11.0
          cache: pnpm
      - run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
      - uses: actions/download-artifact@v7
        with:
          name: ${{ needs.candidate.outputs.artifact }}
          path: release
      - run: pnpm verify:platform

  publish:
    needs: [candidate, platform-witness]
    if: github.event_name == 'push'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 20
    permissions:
      contents: write
      id-token: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v5
        with:
          ref: ${{ github.sha }}
          fetch-depth: 0
      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v5
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v5
        with:
          node-version: 24.11.0
          cache: pnpm
      - run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
      - name: Require npm Trusted Publishing support
        shell: bash
        run: |
          npm --version
          node -e "const {execFileSync}=require('node:child_process');const [major,minor]=execFileSync('npm',['--version'],{encoding:'utf8'}).trim().split('.').map(Number);if(major<11||(major===11&&minor<5))throw new Error('npm 11.5.1 or newer is required for OIDC')"
      - uses: actions/download-artifact@v7
        with:
          name: ${{ needs.candidate.outputs.artifact }}
          path: release
      - id: prepare
        run: node tooling/release/prepare.mjs "${{ github.sha }}"
      - name: Bind verified archives to prepared version
        run: |
          node tooling/release/verify-prepared-candidate.mjs
          pnpm verify:candidates
      - name: Create the release commit and tag
        if: steps.prepare.outputs.mode == 'new'
        shell: bash
        run: |
          git config user.name github-actions[bot]
          git config user.email 41898282+github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com
          node tooling/release/create-release-record.mjs "${{ github.sha }}"
          tags="$(node -e "const c=require('./release/candidate.json');console.log([c.projection.releaseTag,...c.projection.packages.map(p=>p.tag)].map(t=>'refs/tags/'+t).join(' '))")"
          git push --atomic origin HEAD:main $tags
      - name: Persist the exact candidate before publication
        shell: bash
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ github.token }}
        run: |
          tag="${{ steps.prepare.outputs.release_tag }}"
          asset="suite-candidates-${{ github.sha }}.tgz"
          local_asset="release/assets/$asset"
          if ! gh release view "$tag" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
            gh release create "$tag" --verify-tag --title "$tag" --notes "Exact Pi Suite candidate for ${{ github.sha }}"
          fi
          if gh release view "$tag" --json assets --jq '.assets[].name' | grep -Fx "$asset"; then
            rm -rf release/persisted
            mkdir -p release/persisted
            gh release download "$tag" --pattern "$asset" --dir release/persisted
            cmp "$local_asset" "release/persisted/$asset"
          else
            gh release upload "$tag" "$local_asset"
          fi
      - run: pnpm publish:candidates

  public-acceptance:
    needs: [candidate, publish]
    if: github.event_name == 'push'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 20
    permissions:
      contents: read
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v5
        with:
          ref: ${{ github.sha }}
          fetch-depth: 0
      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v5
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v5
        with:
          node-version: 24.11.0
          cache: pnpm
      - run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
      - uses: actions/download-artifact@v7
        with:
          name: ${{ needs.candidate.outputs.artifact }}
          path: release
      - run: pnpm verify:registry

The gap is that those witnesses are not yet built.
findings.claim: In the dossier-time implementation candidate, verify:consumers, Chrome release:check, and the test:release + verify:packages portions of root `pnpm verify` (which for web includes ci:verify/e2e) live ONLY in the local container preflight and are deliberately, test-enforcedly excluded from release.yml. Removing local preflight without first adding authoritative CI owners drops these checks from every supported release path, violating Criterion 3 and the invariant that all preflight checks remain represented in CI.

tooling/release/candidate-pipeline.mjs:17-21 verifyReleaseSource runs only `pnpm release:verify-source`
package.json:19 release:verify-source == `pnpm run check:workspace` (no test:release, no verify:packages)
package.json:27,31 root `verify`=check:workspace && test:release && verify:packages; consumers/chrome only in release:verify + preflight
tooling/release/candidate-pipeline.mjs:48-55 local `full` runs pnpm verify + verifyCandidate + verifyConsumers
tooling/release/container-preflight.mjs:29 runs pi-chrome release:check; :96 runs candidate-pipeline full
tooling/release/workflow.test.mjs:124-125 asserts candidate job has no `pnpm verify` and no `release:check`; :158-166 asserts workflow does NOT contain verify:consumers
Grep .github: only verify:platform, verify:candidates, verify:registry are present in the workflow
findings.falsifier: A refreshed implementation candidate whose workflow assigns authoritative CI jobs to verify:consumers, chrome candidate/smoke, test:release and verify:packages (with the workflow.test.mjs prohibitions inverted), evidenced by the required command-to-job coverage map (Required Evidence #1).
findings.violated_invariant: Criterion 3 / All checks currently supplied by local preflight remain represented in CI (pnpm verify, consumer verification, Chrome candidate/smoke)

findings.causal_boundary: Property of verify-consumers.mjs, web ci:verify, and pi-chrome release:check as currently authored; becomes a blocker only if these cannot be reproduced faithfully on hosted runners. No runner-fidelity measurement (Required Evidence #7) is present.
findings.claim: The checks that must migrate include browser/SSE/CLI consumer smoke and web e2e, which today run on the operator's macOS host and a local Linux container. Their faithful execution on GitHub-hosted ubuntu/macos/windows runners (browsers, services, ports, hardware) is unproven, so the coverage-map closure in F1 is a re-architecture that can hit Abandon Condition #4 (required checks cannot run reliably in Actions), not a one-line addition.
evidence: tooling/release/verify-consumers.mjs:40-50 web archive browser/sse/cli/health/page smoke
tooling/release/verify-consumers.mjs:52-102 combined npm+pnpm real install of archives
tooling/release/workflow.test.mjs:182-185 web ci:verify runs `pnpm test:e2e:run`
tooling/release/container-preflight.mjs:15 preflight asserts darwin (Apple container) host; Chrome release:check runs on host at :29
findings.falsifier: Measured green runs of consumer smoke, web e2e, and Chrome release:check on the intended hosted runners, plus evidence the required browsers/services/ports are faithfully available (Required Evidence #7).

findings.causal_boundary: Describes the release.yml publish job as-is. The vulnerability materializes only under the candidate-ref model if promotion/publication are not split from candidate-code execution; it is not a live exploit in the current push-to-main flow.
findings.claim: The current publish job executes candidate-controlled code (root `prepare` lifecycle on install, prepare.mjs, verify-prepared-candidate.mjs, pnpm verify:candidates, create-release-record.mjs, pnpm publish:candidates) while holding contents:write + id-token:write and pushing HEAD:main. This is acceptable today because the SHA is already on main (push-to-main model), but it is structurally incompatible with proposal C, whose promoter must verify the witness set WITHOUT executing candidate code. Implementing C by retargeting this job at an unmerged candidate ref would re-open the exact failure class (Criterion 5 / Abandon Condition #2).
evidence: .github/workflows/release.yml:144-146 publish permissions contents:write + id-token:write
.github/workflows/release.yml:157 pnpm install runs root `prepare` (package.json:15 effect-tsgo patch)
.github/workflows/release.yml:167-172 runs prepare.mjs, verify-prepared-candidate.mjs, pnpm verify:candidates
.github/workflows/release.yml:176-181 create-release-record.mjs then `git push --atomic origin HEAD:main $tags`
.github/workflows/release.yml:201 pnpm publish:candidates under OIDC; all tooling/release/*.mjs are checked out at github.sha
findings.falsifier: An implementation where a privileged promoter with no dependency install and no candidate-authored code verifies the witness set and fast-forwards main, and publication consumes only pre-witnessed archive bytes through a boundary that never grants candidate code write/OIDC authority (Required Evidence #4).
findings.violated_invariant: Proposal step 4 / Abandon Condition #2: the promoter must not execute candidate-controlled code while holding write or publication authority

findings.causal_boundary: Statement about in-tree evidence only. It does NOT assert server-side branch protection is absent (it may exist); it asserts the exclusivity guarantee is not demonstrable from the repository and must be produced as Required Evidence #3.
findings.claim: Exclusive-promoter enforcement is unverifiable from the repository: `.github` contains only release.yml, with no ruleset-as-code, CODEOWNERS, or branch-protection config in tree. Assumption #3 and Required Evidence #3 are therefore undischarged from available evidence, and Abandon Condition #1 (repo cannot prevent supported direct writes to main/tags) cannot be closed here. Fast-forward promotion is insufficient if any other supported path can push to main or create release tags (Known Weakness #3).
evidence: Glob .github/**/* returns only .github/workflows/release.yml (no ruleset/CODEOWNERS/branch-protection files)
Glob for CODEOWNERS/ruleset/branch-protection finds only node_modules third-party files
push-release.mjs:20 and release.yml:181 both perform `git push` to main from different identities, implying reliance on unseen server-side rules
findings.falsifier: GitHub repository rules/branch protection showing every supported write to main and to release tags passes only through the promoter identity, plus a fail-closed test that attempts a direct-main push and a rogue tag and observes rejection (Required Evidence #3 + #8).
findings.violated_invariant: Criterion 1 / Abandon Condition #1: only witnessed SHAs may reach main; no supported path may bypass the promoter

findings.causal_boundary: Current publish flow. The record is a deterministic derivation re-checked by classify.mjs, so it is managed today; the risk is that the CI-only cutover conflates 'witnessed source' with 'what lands on main' unless the record model is redefined.
findings.claim: The publish job creates a NEW release-record commit (bumped versions) and pushes it to main — a SHA never covered by the candidate/platform witness DAG. Proposal step 4 says main is fast-forwarded to the exact witnessed source SHA, which is in direct tension with advancing main to an unwitnessed release-record commit. The design must keep source SHA, release-record SHA, tag target, and archive digest as distinct-but-bound identities before the local gate is removed.
evidence: .github/workflows/release.yml:173-181 create-release-record.mjs creates a distinct commit pushed to main
tooling/release/prepare.mjs:88-101 writes bumped package versions into the release set
tooling/release/classify.mjs:19-44 independently re-verifies release-record commits (derivation, not witnessed by candidate/platform jobs)
findings.falsifier: A model that either creates the release-record commit before the witness DAG so it becomes the witnessed SHA, or proves via test that the record is a byte-deterministic verified derivation of the witnessed source such that no unwitnessed content reaches main (Evidence That Would Change Our Mind #5).
findings.violated_invariant: Known Weakness #5 / Review Question #2: witnessed source identity vs later release-metadata commit must stay distinct and bound

findings.causal_boundary: release.yml retention and restore logic as written; applies to D's 'reuse an existing witness' claim. For plain C (validate on release request) the constraint is weaker because persistence-before-publish already writes a durable asset.
findings.claim: The candidate run-artifact retention is 14 days, and the 'existing/prior' reuse path depends on either a durable gh release asset or a non-expired run artifact. The optimized continuous-witness form (D) reuses a witnessed archive for a not-yet-released candidate, for which no durable release asset exists yet; after 14 days its only copy expires, forcing a rebuild (new digest) and re-witness. This bounds D's latency benefit and requires an explicit durable-persistence + witness-expiry policy, reinforcing the initial recommendation to prefer on-demand candidate validation (C) over default D.
evidence: .github/workflows/release.yml:111 candidate artifact `retention-days: 14`
.github/workflows/release.yml:73-97 restore depends on durable gh release asset or non-expired prior run artifact
.github/workflows/release.yml:182-200 durable persistence (gh release upload + cmp) only happens at publication time
findings.falsifier: A retention model persisting every eligible witnessed candidate to a durable immutable location at witness time with a defined expiry, and a measurement showing D's saved latency exceeds the added storage/capacity cost (Required Evidence #6 + #7).
findings.violated_invariant: Known Weakness #4: finite retention conflicts with indefinite reuse of a previously witnessed candidate

input.strongest_alternative: Retain Alternative A (mandatory local container preflight) as the sole authoritative gate for this change, and add the missing checks (verify:consumers, Chrome candidate/smoke, test:release, verify:packages) to CI incrementally WITHOUT removing local preflight — cutting over to CI-only only after every precondition is discharged. This is the strongest competitor to adopting the proposal now because F1 shows the remote plane does not currently own the consumer/Chrome/e2e checks (they are test-enforced OUT of the workflow), F3 shows the only privileged job still fuses promotion, publication, and candidate-code execution, and F4 shows exclusive-promoter enforcement is unverifiable in-tree. A's acknowledged weaknesses (duplicated work, operator blocking, dependence on local container runtime) are tolerable during a transition and are strictly safer than deleting the only owner of consumer/e2e/Chrome coverage before CI provably owns it.
surviving_invariants: Architecture C (candidate-ref validation + a privileged promoter that fast-forwards main only after the complete witness set) is the correct target; the challenge does not falsify it, and with F1/F3/F4 preconditions met it does close the unverified-main class.

Only an exact source SHA with a complete witness set may enter main; npm may receive only the exact witnessed archive bytes; no validation result may be reused across a different SHA or archive digest.

Publication must consume the persisted, byte-identical witnessed archive without rebuild or repack (upheld today by release.yml:182-201 cmp/persist-before-publish and verify-prepared-candidate.mjs; same artifact downloaded by macOS/Windows witnesses).
One release source SHA maps to one explicit public-package release set and one exact archive per selected package (Criterion 9).
Source classification stays install-free and cannot gain credentials (classify job has no setup-node/pnpm install; workflow.test.mjs:174-179).
unknowns: Whether GitHub server-side branch protection/rulesets already make the promoter the exclusive writer to main and release tags (not representable in the repo tree).
Whether hosted ubuntu/macos/windows runners faithfully run web `test:e2e:run`, consumer browser/sse/health/cli smoke, and pi-chrome `release:check` (Known Weakness #9; Required Evidence #7 absent).
Measured Actions queue + execution latency versus the local preflight, and whether release urgency requires synchronous completion (Required Evidence #7).

Whether `concurrency.queue: max` (release.yml:10, asserted at workflow.test.mjs:80) is an honored GitHub Actions key or silently ignored — if ignored, promotion serialization under concurrent SHAs / main movement (Criterion 4) may not hold as designed.
The durable-persistence and witness-expiry policy for witnessed-but-unreleased candidates required to make D's reuse claim hold past the 14-day artifact retention (Required Evidence #6).

Assumption #3 and Required Evidence #3 are therefore undischarged from available evidence, and Abandon Condition #1 (repo cannot prevent supported direct writes to main/tags) cannot be closed here. Fast-forward promotion is insufficient if any other supported path can push to main or create release tags (Known Weakness #3).
evidence: Glob .github/**/* returns only .github/workflows/release.yml (no ruleset/CODEOWNERS/branch-protection files)
Glob for CODEOWNERS/ruleset/branch-protection finds only node_modules third-party files
push-release.mjs:20 and release.yml:181 both perform `git push` to main from different identities, implying reliance on unseen server-side rules
findings.falsifier: GitHub repository rules/branch protection showing every supported write to main and to release tags passes only through the promoter identity, plus a fail-closed test that attempts a direct-main push and a rogue tag and observes rejection (Required Evidence #3 + #8).
findings.violated_invariant: Criterion 1 / Abandon Condition #1: only witnessed SHAs may reach main; no supported path may bypass the promoter

findings.causal_boundary: Current publish flow. The record is a deterministic derivation re-checked by classify.mjs, so it is managed today; the risk is that the CI-only cutover conflates 'witnessed source' with 'what lands on main' unless the record model is redefined.
findings.claim: The publish job creates a NEW release-record commit (bumped versions) and pushes it to main — a SHA never covered by the candidate/platform witness DAG. Proposal step 4 says main is fast-forwarded to the exact witnessed source SHA, which is in direct tension with advancing main to an unwitnessed release-record commit. The design must keep source SHA, release-record SHA, tag target, and archive digest as distinct-but-bound identities before the local gate is removed.
evidence: .github/workflows/release.yml:173-181 create-release-record.mjs creates a distinct commit pushed to main
tooling/release/prepare.mjs:88-101 writes bumped package versions into the release set
tooling/release/classify.mjs:19-44 independently re-verifies release-record commits (derivation, not witnessed by candidate/platform jobs)
findings.falsifier: A model that either creates the release-record commit before the witness DAG so it becomes the witnessed SHA, or proves via test that the record is a byte-deterministic verified derivation of the witnessed source such that no unwitnessed content reaches main (Evidence That Would Change Our Mind #5).
findings.violated_invariant: Known Weakness #5 / Review Question #2: witnessed source identity vs later release-metadata commit must stay distinct and bound

findings.causal_boundary: release.yml retention and restore logic as written; applies to D's 'reuse an existing witness' claim. For plain C (validate on release request) the constraint is weaker because persistence-before-publish already writes a durable asset.
findings.claim: The candidate run-artifact retention is 14 days, and the 'existing/prior' reuse path depends on either a durable gh release asset or a non-expired run artifact. The optimized continuous-witness form (D) reuses a witnessed archive for a not-yet-released candidate, for which no durable release asset exists yet; after 14 days its only copy expires, forcing a rebuild (new digest) and re-witness. This bounds D's latency benefit and requires an explicit durable-persistence + witness-expiry policy, reinforcing the initial recommendation to prefer on-demand candidate validation (C) over default D.
evidence: .github/workflows/release.yml:111 candidate artifact `retention-days: 14`
.github/workflows/release.yml:73-97 restore depends on durable gh release asset or non-expired prior run artifact
.github/workflows/release.yml:182-200 durable persistence (gh release upload + cmp) only happens at publication time
findings.falsifier: A retention model persisting every eligible witnessed candidate to a durable immutable location at witness time with a defined expiry, and a measurement showing D's saved latency exceeds the added storage/capacity cost (Required Evidence #6 + #7).
findings.violated_invariant: Known Weakness #4: finite retention conflicts with indefinite reuse of a previously witnessed candidate

structured_output.strongest_alternative: Retain Alternative A (mandatory local container preflight) as the sole authoritative gate for this change, and add the missing checks (verify:consumers, Chrome candidate/smoke, test:release, verify:packages) to CI incrementally WITHOUT removing local preflight — cutting over to CI-only only after every precondition is discharged. This is the strongest competitor to adopting the proposal now because F1 shows the remote plane does not currently own the consumer/Chrome/e2e checks (they are test-enforced OUT of the workflow), F3 shows the only privileged job still fuses promotion, publication, and candidate-code execution, and F4 shows exclusive-promoter enforcement is unverifiable in-tree. A's acknowledged weaknesses (duplicated work, operator blocking, dependence on local container runtime) are tolerable during a transition and are strictly safer than deleting the only owner of consumer/e2e/Chrome coverage before CI provably owns it.
surviving_invariants: Architecture C (candidate-ref validation + a privileged promoter that fast-forwards main only after the complete witness set) is the correct target; the challenge does not falsify it, and with F1/F3/F4 preconditions met it does close the unverified-main class.

Only an exact source SHA with a complete witness set may enter main; npm may receive only the exact witnessed archive bytes; no validation result may be reused across a different SHA or archive digest.

Publication must consume the persisted, byte-identical witnessed archive without rebuild or repack (upheld today by release.yml:182-201 cmp/persist-before-publish and verify-prepared-candidate.mjs; same artifact downloaded by macOS/Windows witnesses).
One release source SHA maps to one explicit public-package release set and one exact archive per selected package (Criterion 9).
Source classification stays install-free and cannot gain credentials (classify job has no setup-node/pnpm install; workflow.test.mjs:174-179).
unknowns: Whether GitHub server-side branch protection/rulesets already make the promoter the exclusive writer to main and release tags (not representable in the repo tree).
Whether hosted ubuntu/macos/windows runners faithfully run web `test:e2e:run`, consumer browser/sse/health/cli smoke, and pi-chrome `release:check` (Known Weakness #9; Required Evidence #7 absent).
Measured Actions queue + execution latency versus the local preflight, and whether release urgency requires synchronous completion (Required Evidence #7).

Whether `concurrency.queue: max` (release.yml:10, asserted at workflow.test.mjs:80) is an honored GitHub Actions key or silently ignored — if ignored, promotion serialization under concurrent SHAs / main movement (Criterion 4) may not hold as designed.
The durable-persistence and witness-expiry policy for witnessed-but-unreleased candidates required to make D's reuse claim hold past the 14-day artifact retention (Required Evidence #6).

findings.causal_boundary: Describes the release.yml publish job as-is. The vulnerability materializes only under the candidate-ref model if promotion/publication are not split from candidate-code execution; it is not a live exploit in the current push-to-main flow.

This is acceptable today because the SHA is already on main (push-to-main model), but it is structurally incompatible with proposal C, whose promoter must verify the witness set WITHOUT executing candidate code. Implementing C by retargeting this job at an unmerged candidate ref would re-open the exact failure class (Criterion 5 / Abandon Condition #2).

findings.causal_boundary: Current publish flow. The record is a deterministic derivation re-checked by classify.mjs, so it is managed today; the risk is that the CI-only cutover conflates 'witnessed source' with 'what lands on main' unless the record model is redefined.

findings.causal_boundary: release.yml retention and restore logic as written; applies to D's 'reuse an existing witness' claim. For plain C (validate on release request) the constraint is weaker because persistence-before-publish already writes a durable asset.
findings.claim: The candidate run-artifact retention is 14 days, and the 'existing/prior' reuse path depends on either a durable gh release asset or a non-expired run artifact. The optimized continuous-witness form (D) reuses a witnessed archive for a not-yet-released candidate, for which no durable release asset exists yet; after 14 days its only copy expires, forcing a rebuild (new digest) and re-witness. This bounds D's latency benefit and requires an explicit durable-persistence + witness-expiry policy, reinforcing the initial recommendation to prefer on-demand candidate validation (C) over default D.

structured_output.strongest_alternative: Retain Alternative A (mandatory local container preflight) as the sole authoritative gate for this change, and add the missing checks (verify:consumers, Chrome candidate/smoke, test:release, verify:packages) to CI incrementally WITHOUT removing local preflight — cutting over to CI-only only after every precondition is discharged. This is the strongest competitor to adopting the proposal now because F1 shows the remote plane does not currently own the consumer/Chrome/e2e checks (they are test-enforced OUT of the workflow), F3 shows the only privileged job still fuses promotion, publication, and candidate-code execution, and F4 shows exclusive-promoter enforcement is unverifiable in-tree. A's acknowledged weaknesses (duplicated work, operator blocking, dependence on local container runtime) are tolerable during a transition and are strictly safer than deleting the only owner of consumer/e2e/Chrome coverage before CI provably owns it.
